elements.sent-2411-002: In this paper, we introduce \pname, a highly versatile and parameter-efficient framework that integrates image conditions into pre-trained Diffusion Transformer (DiT) models.
elements.sent-2411-003: At its core, \pname \ leverages a parameter reuse mechanism, enabling the DiT to encode image conditions using itself as a powerful backbone and process them with its flexible multi-modal attention processors.
elements.sent-2411-004: Unlike existing methods, which rely heavily on additional encoder modules with complex architectures, \pname~(1) effectively and efficiently incorporates injected image conditions with only $~0.1$\% additional parameters, and (2) addresses a wide range of image conditioning tasks in a unified manner, including subject-driven generation and spatially-aligned conditions such as edges, depth, and more.
elements.sent-2411-005: Remarkably, these capabilities are achieved by training on images generated by the DiT itself, which is particularly beneficial for subject-driven generation.
elements.sent-2411-006: Extensive evaluations demonstrate that \pname~outperforms existing UNet-based and DiT-adapted models in both subject-driven and spatially-aligned conditional generation.
elements.sent-2411-007: Additionally, we release our training dataset, Subjects200K, a diverse collection of over 200,000 identity-consistent images, along with an efficient data synthesis pipeline to advance research in subject-consistent generation.
elements.sent-2411-008: Diffusion models\cite{ho2020denoising, rombach2021highresolution, podell2023sdxl have revolutionized the field of visual generation, demonstrating remarkable capabilities that significantly outperform traditional approaches like Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)\cite{goodfellow2020generative in terms of image quality and diversity.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: While these models excel at generating highly realistic imagery, a critical challenge persists: enabling precise and flexible control over the generation process to accommodate diverse and complex user requirements.
elements.sent-2411-010: Text-based conditioning has been a cornerstone in advancing controllable generation\cite{rombach2021highresolution, podell2023sdxl, blackforestlabs_flux, chen2023pixart, peebles2023scalable, tian2024visual , offering an intuitive interface for users to specify their desired outputs.}
elements.sent-2411-011: However, text prompts alone often fail to convey precise spatial details and structural attributes that users wish to control.
elements.sent-2411-012: Consequently, recent research has explored complementary conditioning modalities for guiding diffusion models, with image-based control emerging as a particularly effective approach\cite{zhang2023adding, ye2023ip, mou2024t2i, zhao2024uni, li2024blip .}
elements.sent-2411-013: This multi-modal conditioning strategy enables more detailed and accurate control over the generation process, addressing the limitations inherent in purely text-based interfaces.
elements.sent-2411-014: Current image conditioning methods can be broadly categorized into spatially aligned and non-spatially aligned approaches.
elements.sent-2411-015: Spatially aligned tasks such as sketch-to-image and inpainting require direct correspondence between conditioning and output images, typically achieved through methods like ControlNet\cite{zhang2023adding that inject conditioning features in a spatially-preserving manner.}
elements.sent-2411-016: In contrast, non-spatially aligned applications including subject driven generation and style transfer, as demonstrated by IP-Adapter\cite{ye2023ip , often employ pre-trained encoders like CLIP\cite{radford2021learning to extract global features for integration via cross-attention mechanisms.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Despite the effectiveness of existing image-conditioned approaches, they present several limitations that hinder their efficiency and flexibility~\cite{zhang2023adding, ye2023ip, mou2024t2i .}
elements.sent-2411-018: First, most existing methods are designed specifically for UNet-based architectures~\cite{ronneberger2015u, zhang2023adding, mou2024t2i, ye2023ip, ruiz2023dreambooth, zhang2024ssr, zhao2024uni, zavadski2023controlnet, peng2024controlnext, li2025controlnet , as seen in Stable Diffusion models~\cite{rombach2021highresolution, podell2023sdxl .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: While these approaches work well with UNet's encoder-decoder structure, they may not translate effectively to the more advanced Diffusion Transformer (DiT) models\cite{peebles2023scalable that have demonstrated superior image generation quality\cite{blackforestlabs_flux, chen2023pixart .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Additionally, current approaches typically specialize in either spatially aligned~\cite{mou2024t2i, zhao2024uni, zhang2023adding or non-spatially aligned tasks~\cite{ye2023ip, zhang2024ssr, li2024photomaker, li2024blip, kumari2023multi , lacking a unified architecture to handle both control types effectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: This specialization often requires practitioners to employ different methods for different control scenarios, increasing system complexity and implementation overhead.
elements.sent-2411-022: Furthermore, these methods rely heavily on additional network structures~\cite{ye2023ip, zhang2024ssr, mou2024t2i, zhao2024uni, zhang2023adding, li2024photomaker , which introduce substantial parameter overhead.}
elements.sent-2411-023: To address these limitations, we propose a parameter-efficient approach for incorporating image-based control into DiT architectures\cite{peebles2023scalable .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Our method reuse the model's existing VAE encoder\cite{rombach2021highresolution to process conditioning images.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Following the same token processing pipeline as noisy image tokens, we augment the encoded features with learnable position embeddings\cite{su2024roformer , and seamlessly integrate them alongside latent noise in the denoising network.}
elements.sent-2411-026: This design enables direct multi-modal attention interactions\cite{peebles2023scalable, ruan2023mm between condition and generation tokens throughout the DiT's transformer blocks, facilitating efficient information exchange and control signal propagation.}

elements.sent-2411-027: We implemented our method on the high-performing DiT-structured diffusion model, FLUX.1-dev\cite{blackforestlabs_flux , a large-scale model containing 12 billion parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Extensive experiments on edge-guided generation, depth-aware synthesis, region-specific editing, and identity-preserving generation indicate that our DiT-based approach yields better results compared to both UNet-based implementations\cite{zhang2023adding, ye2023ip, jackyhate2024t2i and their community adaptations on the FLUX.1 model~\cite{flux1controlnet2024, fluxipadapter2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: For identity-preserving generation, we developed a novel data synthesis pipeline that generates high-quality, identity-consistent image pairs.
elements.sent-2411-030: Using this pipeline, we created a comprehensive dataset comprising over 200,000 diverse images.
elements.sent-2411-031: To facilitate future research in this direction, we will release both our dataset and the complete pipeline implementation as open-source resources\footnote{More details are available in the supplementary material.. .}
elements.sent-2411-032: In summary, we highlight our contributions as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-033: We present a parameter-efficient method for enabling image-conditioned control in Diffusion Transformer (DiT) models, achieving both spatially aligned and non-spatially aligned control within a unified framework.
elements.sent-2411-034: We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through extensive experiments across diverse control tasks, including edge-guided generation, depth-aware synthesis, region-specific editing, and identity-preserving generation, consistently outperforming existing methods on both UNet implementations and their DiT adaptations.
elements.sent-2411-035: We develop and release \datasetname, a high-quality dataset of over 200,000 subject-consistent images, along with an efficient data synthesis pipeline, providing valuable resources to the research community for further exploration of subject-consistent generation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-036: Diffusion-based methods have emerged as a powerful framework for image generation\cite{ho2020denoising, rombach2021highresolution , demonstrating success across diverse tasks including text-to-image synthesis~\cite{rombach2021highresolution, chen2023pixart, saharia2022photorealistic , image-to-image translation~\cite{saharia2022palette , and image editing~\cite{meng2021sdedit, avrahami2022blended .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Recent advances have led to significant improvements in both quality and efficiency, notably through the introduction of latent diffusion models~\cite{rombach2021highresolution .}
elements.sent-2411-038: To further enhance generative capabilities, large-scale transformer architectures have been integrated into these frameworks, leading to advanced models like DiT\cite{peebles2023scalable, chen2023pixart, blackforestlabs_flux, chen2024pixart .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Building on these architectural innovations, FLUX\cite{blackforestlabs_flux incorporates transformer-based design with flow matching objectives~\cite{lipman2022flow , achieving state-of-the-art generation performance.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Controllable generation has been extensively studied in the context of diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-041: Text-to-image models\cite{rombach2021highresolution, podell2023sdxl have established a foundation for conditional generation, while various approaches have been developed to incorporate additional control signals such as image.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Notable methods include ControlNet~\cite{zhang2023adding , enabling spatially aligned control in diffusion models, and T2I-Adapter~\cite{mou2024t2i , which improves efficiency with lightweight adapters.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: UniControl~\cite{qin2023unicontrol uses Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) to unify different spatial conditions, further reducing model size.}
elements.sent-2411-044: However, these methods rely on spatially adding condition information to the denoising network's hidden states, inherently limiting their effectiveness for non-spatial tasks like subject-driven generation.
elements.sent-2411-045: IP-Adapter~\cite{ye2023ip addresses this by introducing cross-attention through an additional encoder, and SSR-Encoder~\cite{zhang2024ssr further enhances identity preservation in image-conditioned tasks.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Despite these advances~\cite{li2024blip, ma2024subject, gal2022image , a unified solution for both spatially aligned and non-aligned tasks remains elusive.}
elements.sent-2411-047: The Diffusion Transformer (DiT) model~\cite{peebles2023scalable , employed in architectures like FLUX.1~\cite{blackforestlabs_flux , Stable Diffusion 3~\cite{rombach2021highresolution , and PixArt~\cite{chen2023pixart , uses a denoising network of transformer blocks to refine noisy image tokens iteratively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Each transformer block processes two types of tokens: noisy image tokens \( \mathbf{X \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times d \) and text condition tokens \( \mathbf{C _{\text{T \in \mathbb{R ^{M \times d \), where \( d \) is the embedding dimension, \( N \) and \( M \) are the number of image and text tokens respectively (Figure~\ref{fig:method ).}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: These tokens are embedded into hidden states \( X \) and \( C_{\text{T \), which maintain consistent shapes throughout the transformer blocks.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: In each DiT block, after normalizing \( X \) and \( C_{\text{T \), they are processed by the core MM-Attention module~\cite{ruan2023mm , which employs Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE)~\cite{su2024roformer to incorporate positional dependencies across tokens.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: For a token at position \( (i, j) \) in the 2D grid, RoPE applies rotation matrices to the query and key projections: \begin{equation}Q_{X}(i, j) = W_{Q} \left( X_{i, j} \cdot R(i, j) \right), K_{X}(i, j) = W_{K} \left( X_{i, j} \cdot R(i, j) \right),\end{equation} where \( R(i, j) \) is the rotation matrix at position \( (i, j) \).
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}Q_{X}(i, j) = W_{Q} \left( X_{i, j} \cdot R(i, j) \right), K_{X}(i, j) = W_{K} \left( X_{i, j} \cdot R(i, j) \right),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-052: Similarly, the text condition tokens \( C_{\text{T \) have their query and key projections defined in the same way, with all text token positions set to \( (0,0) \) in FLUX.1.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: After applying RoPE, the queries, keys, and values from both token types are concatenated to form unified matrices \( Q_Z \), \( K_Z \), and \( V_Z \), representing the combined token set \( Z = [X; C_{\text{T ] \).}}
elements.sent-2411-054: The MM-Attention operation is then computed as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:attn} \text{MMAttention}(Z) = \text{softmax} \left( \frac{Q_Z K_Z^\top}{\sqrt{d}} \right) V_Z,\end{equation} enabling interactions between image and text condition tokens through the attention mechanism.

elements.sent-2411-055: Our approach first encodes the condition image through the model's VAE, projecting it into the same latent space as the noisy image tokens to form \( \mathbf{C _{\text{I \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times d \).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Previous methods like ControlNet~\cite{zhang2023adding and T2I-Adapter~\cite{mou2024t2i incorporate the condition image by spatially aligning and adding its hidden states directly to those of the noisy image tokens: \begin{equation}H_{X} = H_{X} + H_{C_{\text{I}}},\end{equation} where \( H_{X \) represents the combined hidden states for further processing, with \( H_{C_{\text{I \) being the hidden states from the condition image.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: While this approach proves effective for spatially aligned tasks, it faces two key limitations: (1) it lacks flexibility when handling non-aligned scenarios, and (2) even in spatially aligned cases, the direct addition of hidden states constrains token interactions, potentially limiting the model's performance.

elements.sent-2411-062: The integration of condition images requires careful consideration of positional information to ensure effective interaction between condition and target images.
elements.sent-2411-063: Previous methods often assume rigid spatial alignment between condition and output images, limiting their applicability to non-aligned tasks.
elements.sent-2411-064: Moreover, the relative positioning between condition and target tokens can significantly impact the model's learning efficiency and generalization ability.
elements.sent-2411-065: Leveraging our unified sequence design where condition tokens are concatenated with other tokens, we have the flexibility to explore different positional encoding strategies.
elements.sent-2411-066: In FLUX.1's Transformers, each token is assigned a corresponding position index to encode spatial information.
elements.sent-2411-067: For a 512$\times$512 input image, the VAE~\cite{kingma2013auto encoder first projects it into the latent space, then the latent representation is divided into a 32$\times$32 grid of tokens, where each token receives a unique two-dimensional position index \((i, j)\) with \( i, j \in [0, 31] \).}
elements.sent-2411-068: This indexing scheme preserves the spatial structure of the original image in the latent space, while text tokens maintain a fixed position index of \((0, 0)\).
elements.sent-2411-069: For spatially aligned tasks, our initial approach was to assign condition tokens the same position embeddings as their corresponding tokens in the original image.
elements.sent-2411-070: However, for non-spatially aligned tasks such as subject-driven generation, our experiments revealed that shifting the position indices of condition tokens leads to faster convergence (Figure~\ref{fig:pos_loss1 ).}
elements.sent-2411-071: Specifically, we shift the condition image tokens to indices \((i, j)\) where \( i \in [0, 31]\) and \( j \in [32, 64]\), ensuring no spatial overlap with the original image tokens \( X \).
elements.sent-2411-072: The unified attention mechanism we adopt not only enables flexible token interaction but also allows us to precisely control the influence of condition images.
elements.sent-2411-073: Specifically, we designed a method that allows for manual adjustment of the condition image’s effect during inference.
elements.sent-2411-074: For a given strength factor \( \gamma \), setting \( \gamma = 0 \) removes the condition image's influence, resulting in an output based purely on the original input.
elements.sent-2411-075: At \( \gamma = 1 \), the output fully reflects the condition image, and as \( \gamma \) increases beyond 1, the condition's effect becomes even more pronounced.
elements.sent-2411-076: To achieve this controllability, we introduce a bias term into the original MM-Attention operation.
elements.sent-2411-077: Specifically, we modify Equation~\ref{eq:attn to: \begin{equation}\text{BiasedAttention}(Z) = \text{softmax} \left( \frac{Q_Z K_Z^\top}{\sqrt{d}} + \text{bias}(\gamma) \right) V_Z,\end{equation} where bias\(\gamma\) is designed to adjust the attention weights between condition tokens and other tokens based on the strength factor \(\gamma\).}
elements.sent-2411-078: The bias term is constructed as a \((M+2N) \times (M+2N)\) matrix, where \(M\) is the number of text tokens, and \(N\) is the number of noisy image tokens and condition image tokens each.
elements.sent-2411-079: The matrix has the following structure: \begin{equation}\text{bias}(\gamma) = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{0}_{M\times M} \mathbf{0}_{M\times N} \mathbf{0}_{M\times N} \mathbf{0}_{N\times M} \mathbf{0}_{N\times N} \log(\gamma)\mathbf{1}_{N\times N} \mathbf{0}_{N\times M} \log(\gamma)\mathbf{1}_{N\times N} \mathbf{0}_{N\times N} \end{bmatrix}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}bias(\gamma) =  0_{M\times M} 0_{M\times N} 0_{M\times N} 0_{N\times M} 0_{N\times N} \log(\gamma)1_{N\times N} 0_{N\times M} \log(\gamma)1_{N\times N} 0_{N\times N} .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-080: This design ensures that the strength factor \(\gamma\) only affects the attention weights between noisy image tokens and condition image tokens, while maintaining the original attention patterns for text tokens and within-modality interactions.

elements.sent-2411-081: Training models for subject-consistent generation typically requires paired images that maintain identity consistency while exhibiting variations in pose, lighting, and other attributes.
elements.sent-2411-082: Previous methods like IP-Adapter~\cite{ye2023ip use identical images for conditioning and target pairs, which proves effective for their approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-083: However, in our framework, this setup leads to overfitting, causing the model to generate outputs nearly identical to the inputs.
elements.sent-2411-084: To overcome these limitations, we developed a dataset featuring images that preserve subject identity while incorporating natural variations.
elements.sent-2411-085: While existing datasets~\cite{ruiz2023dreambooth, kumari2023multi, li2024blip, li2024photomaker address similar needs, they often face constraints in either quality or scale.}
elements.sent-2411-086: We therefore propose a novel synthesis pipeline leveraging FLUX's inherent capability to generate pairs of visually related images from carefully crafted prompts.
elements.sent-2411-087: Our pipeline utilizes ChatGPT-4o to generate over 20,000 diverse image descriptions, which guide FLUX in producing more than 200,000 images (Figure~\ref{fig:dataset ).}
elements.sent-2411-088: The generated images undergo quality assessment using ChatGPT-4o's vision capabilities, ensuring both consistency and diversity in the final dataset.

elements.sent-2411-154: Our dataset generation process consists of three main stages: description generation, image synthesis, and quality assessment.
elements.sent-2411-155: Description Generation We employed ChatGPT-4o to create a hierarchical structure of descriptions: We first generated 42 diverse object categories, including furniture, vehicles, electronics, clothing, and others.

elements.sent-2411-157: Each object entry consists of: (1) A brief description, (2) Eight diverse scene descriptions, (3) One studio photo description.
elements.sent-2411-158: Figure~\ref{lst:example shows a representative example of our structured description format.}
elements.sent-2411-159: Image Synthesis We designed a prompt template to leverage FLUX's capability of generating paired images containing the same subject.
elements.sent-2411-160: Our template synthesizes a comprehensive prompt by combining a brief object description with two distinct scene descriptions, ensuring subject consistency while introducing environmental variations.
elements.sent-2411-161: The detailed prompt structure is illustrated in Figure~\ref{lst:img_prompt .}

elements.sent-2411-168: Subject consistency: Ensuring the subject maintains identity across both views.
elements.sent-2411-169: Image quality: Confirming high resolution and visual fidelity.

elements.sent-2411-203: The ability to generate high-quality images with precise user control remains a central challenge in computer vision.
elements.sent-2411-204: While diffusion models~\cite{ho2020denoising, rombach2021highresolution have significantly advanced the state of the art in image generation, surpassing traditional GAN-based approaches~\cite{goodfellow2020generative in both quality and diversity, the problem of fine-grained control persists.}}
elements.sent-2411-205: Text-conditioned diffusion models~\cite{rombach2021highresolution, podell2023sdxl, esser2024scaling,blackforestlabs_flux serve as the primary paradigm for controllable generation, enabling users to guide the synthesis process through natural language descriptions.}
elements.sent-2411-206: However, these models fundamentally lack the capacity to specify exact spatial details and visual attributes that users often need to control.
elements.sent-2411-207: To address this limitation, recent works have turned to image-based control methods~\cite{zhang2023adding, ye2023ip, mou2024t2i, zhao2024uni, li2024blip, shi2024instantbooth,xiao2024fastcomposer, li2024photomaker, qin2023unicontrol, wei2023elite as a promising solution to achieve fine-grained control.}
elements.sent-2411-208: These approaches enable users to specify their intentions through reference images or visual hints, offering more precise guidance than text alone~\cite{rombach2021highresolution .}
elements.sent-2411-209: However, current image control methods face several key challenges: First, existing methods require substantial architectural modifications with dedicated control modules, leading to significant parameter overhead~\cite{zhang2023adding, ye2023ip .}
elements.sent-2411-210: Second, these approaches show clear task bias -- they typically work well for either spatially aligned controls (e.g., edge-guided or depth-guided generation~\cite{zhang2023adding,mou2024t2i,zhao2024uni,qin2023unicontrol ) or spatially unaligned ones (e.g., style transfer or subject-driven generation~\cite{ye2023ip, li2024photomaker, li2024blip, shi2024instantbooth ), but rarely both.}}
elements.sent-2411-211: Third, current approaches are built primarily for UNet architectures~\cite{ronneberger2015u .}
elements.sent-2411-212: These approaches may yield suboptimal results when applied to newer Diffusion Transformer (DiT) models~\cite{peebles2023scalable due to fundamental architectural differences, despite the latter's superior generation capabilities~\cite{blackforestlabs_flux, chen2023pixart, esser2024scaling .}}
elements.sent-2411-213: These challenges, particularly within the emerging paradigm of Diffusion Transformer models, motivate us to rethink the fundamental approach to image control.
elements.sent-2411-214: We propose \pname, an omni-capable yet minimal framework that achieves effective control by leveraging existing components within the DiT architecture.
elements.sent-2411-215: To achieve the goal of minimal architectural and parameters overhead, {\pname first utilizes the built-in VAE encoder to project conditioning images into the same latent space as the target images.}
elements.sent-2411-216: The conditional tokens are concatenated with the noisy latent and processed by the DiT using multi-modal attention~\cite{ruan2023mm, peebles2023scalable .}
elements.sent-2411-217: This unified sequence allows the denoising network to handle both signals directly, requiring only minimal LoRA~\cite{devalal2018lora fine-tuning of the existing transformer blocks.}
elements.sent-2411-218: Unlike existing methods such as IP-Adapter~\cite{ye2023ip that relies on separate CLIP~\cite{radford2021learning feature extractors and ControlNet~\cite{flux1controlnet2024 that requires additional control modules, {\pname significantly reduces architectural complexity and parameter overhead.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-308: We present OminiControl, a novel approach that rethinks how image conditions are integrated into Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architectures.
elements.sent-2411-309: Current image conditioning methods either introduce substantial parameter overhead or handle only specific control tasks effectively, limiting their practical versatility.
elements.sent-2411-310: OminiControl addresses these limitations through three key innovations: (1) a minimal architectural design that leverages the DiT's own VAE encoder and transformer blocks, requiring just 0.1\% additional parameters; (2) a unified sequence processing strategy that combines condition tokens with image tokens for flexible token interactions; and (3) a dynamic position encoding mechanism that adapts to both spatially-aligned and non-aligned control tasks.
elements.sent-2411-311: Our extensive experiments show that this streamlined approach not only matches but surpasses the performance of specialized methods across multiple conditioning tasks.
elements.sent-2411-312: To overcome data limitations in subject-driven generation, we also introduce Subjects200K, a large-scale dataset of identity-consistent image pairs synthesized using DiT models themselves.
elements.sent-2411-313: This work demonstrates that effective image control can be achieved without architectural complexity, opening new possibilities for efficient and versatile image generation systems.
elements.sent-2411-314: While diffusion models~\cite{ho2020denoising, rombach2021highresolution have significantly advanced image generation, surpassing traditional GAN-based approaches~\cite{goodfellow2020generative in both quality and diversity, fine-grained control remains problematic.}}
elements.sent-2411-315: Text-conditioned models~\cite{rombach2021highresolution, podell2023sdxl, esser2024scaling, blackforestlabs_flux serve as the primary controllable generation paradigm, yet fundamentally lack the capacity to specify exact spatial details and visual attributes that users often need.}
elements.sent-2411-316: To address this limitation, some works have explored image-based control methods~\cite{zhang2023adding, ye2023ip, mou2024t2i, zhao2024uni, li2024blip, shi2024instantbooth, xiao2024fastcomposer, li2024photomaker, qin2023unicontrol, wei2023elite, li2025controlarcontrollableimagegeneration, sun2024anycontrolcreateartworkversatile, he2024dynamiccontroladaptiveconditionselection, bar2023multidiffusion,lin2024ctrlxcontrollingstructureappearance, hua2023dreamtunersingleimagesubjectdriven, he2025anystoryunifiedsinglemultiple, li2023blip, rout2024semantic, xiao2024fastcomposer, wang2024instantid , enabling users to specify their intentions through reference images or visual hints, offering more precise guidance than text alone~\cite{rombach2021highresolution .}}
elements.sent-2411-317: However, current image control methods face several key challenges: First, existing methods require substantial architectural modifications with dedicated control modules~\cite{zhang2023adding, ye2023ip, pan2023kosmos .}
elements.sent-2411-318: Second, these approaches show clear task bias -- they typically work well for either spatially aligned controls (e.g., edge-guided or depth-guided generation~\cite{zhang2023adding,mou2024t2i,zhao2024uni,qin2023unicontrol, li2025controlarcontrollableimagegeneration, sun2024anycontrolcreateartworkversatile, he2024dynamiccontroladaptiveconditionselection, bar2023multidiffusion,lin2024ctrlxcontrollingstructureappearance ) or spatially unaligned ones (e.g., style transfer or subject-driven generation~\cite{ye2023ip, li2024photomaker, li2024blip, shi2024instantbooth, hua2023dreamtunersingleimagesubjectdriven}\cite{he2025anystoryunifiedsinglemultiple, ma2024characteradapterpromptguidedregioncontrol ), but rarely both.}}
elements.sent-2411-319: These approaches yield suboptimal results when applied to newer Diffusion Transformer (DiT) models~\cite{peebles2023scalable due to fundamental architectural differences, despite the latter's superior generation capabilities~\cite{blackforestlabs_flux, chen2023pixart, esser2024scaling .}}
elements.sent-2411-320: We propose \pname, an omni-capable yet minimal framework that achieves effective and flexible control.
elements.sent-2411-321: For minimal architecture, {\pname employs a parameter reuse strategy that leverages DiT's existing components—particularly its VAE encoder and transformer blocks—which already possess the necessary capabilities to process visual control signals.}
elements.sent-2411-322: By reusing these components with minimal fine-tuning, our approach dramatically reduces parameter overhead while maintaining control effectiveness, requiring only 0.1\% additional parameters compared to the base model.
elements.sent-2411-323: To achieve omni-capability across diverse tasks, {\pname introduces a unified sequence processing approach that fundamentally differs from previous methods' rigid feature addition.}
elements.sent-2411-324: By directly concatenating condition tokens with noisy image tokens in a unified sequence, we allow the multi-modal attention mechanism to discover appropriate relationships between tokens—whether spatial or semantic—without imposing artificial constraints.
elements.sent-2411-325: This flexibility is crucial for handling both spatially-aligned tasks like edge-guided generation and non-aligned tasks like subject-driven generation within a single framework.
elements.sent-2411-326: Building on this unified approach, {\pname incorporates a dynamic positioning strategy that adaptively assigns position indices based on the task type, enabling true omni-capability without task-specific architectural modifications.}
elements.sent-2411-327: Furthermore, for practical flexibility, we introduce an attention bias mechanism that allows users to dynamically adjust the influence of image conditions at inference time, providing crucial control over the generation process.
elements.sent-2411-328: The effectiveness of our architecture depends on high-quality training data, particularly for subject-driven generation.
elements.sent-2411-329: Recognizing that state-of-the-art DiT models can generate remarkably consistent image pairs of the same subject, we develop an automated data synthesis pipeline that creates Subjects200K—a dataset of over 200,000 diverse, identity-consistent images that provides the rich training signals needed to fully realize {\pname 's potential.}
elements.sent-2411-330: We propose {\pname , a minimal and universal control framework for DiT models that requires only 0.1\% additional parameters while effectively handling both spatially-aligned and non-aligned tasks, demonstrating that extensive architectural modifications are unnecessary for effective image conditioning.}
elements.sent-2411-331: We identify two key technical innovations that enable omni-capability: (1) unified sequence processing that outperforms traditional feature addition, and (2) adaptive position encoding that strategically assigns position indices based on task requirements.
elements.sent-2411-332: We design a flexible attention bias mechanism that allows precise adjustment of conditioning strength at inference time, enhancing practical control within the multi-modal framework without compromising performance.
elements.sent-2411-333: We develop and release Subjects200K, a large-scale dataset containing over 200,000 identity-consistent images to advance future research.
elements.sent-2411-334: Despite these advances~\cite{li2024blip, ma2024subject, gal2022image, pan2023kosmos,ruiz2023dreambooth, hua2023dreamtunersingleimagesubjectdriven, kumari2023multi, he2025anystoryunifiedsinglemultiple, ma2024characteradapterpromptguidedregioncontrol , a unified solution for both spatially aligned and non-aligned tasks remains elusive.}
